stations.label: THE FIRST BREATH OF UNITY
stations.line: I was born from many broken currencies into one — the People's Bank gathered the fragments the war had left, and out of that arithmetic of exhaustion, I became a single voice.
stations.era_photo: A crowded Beiping banking hall in winter 1948, clerks in padded cotton jackets counting freshly printed one-yuan notes; grainy black-and-white silver gelatin print, slightly overexposed, the paper edges already curling with age.

stations.label: THE SECOND ISSUE, THE CLEAN SLATE
stations.line: This is a moment with two truths, and I will give you both: the old notes had swollen to numbers no one could hold, so they revalued me ten-thousand to one — I was reborn as something legible.
stations.era_photo: A state print works in 1955 with rows of pastel new-series banknotes hanging to dry, workers in blue Mao-collar uniforms inspecting sheets; black-and-white silver gelatin, deep shadow, propaganda-poster crispness in the framing.

stations.label: THE DOOR OPENS A CRACK
stations.line: For thirty years I spoke only inside my own house; then the reforms began, and for the first time I was asked to stand at the threshold and greet the foreign visitor — I had to learn to translate myself.
stations.era_photo: A Shenzhen special economic zone office in 1979, foreign trade certificates and FEC vouchers on a wooden desk beside an early adding machine; faded Kodachrome color slide, warm amber cast, sunlight through metal window blinds.

stations.label: THE TWO PRICES BECOME ONE
stations.line: Precisely here I stopped living a double life — the official rate and the swap-market rate were unified, and I understood for the first time what it costs to be one thing to everyone at once.
stations.era_photo: A Shanghai foreign exchange trading floor in early 1994, traders in shirtsleeves at boxy CRT terminals showing green digits, cigarette smoke in the air; early digital point-and-shoot photograph, slight motion blur, saturated fluorescent lighting.

stations.label: THE PEG LOOSENS ITS GRIP
stations.line: They let me move — a managed float against a basket, small and careful, but movement all the same — and the international room finally leaned in to listen to me directly.
stations.era_photo: A PBOC press briefing podium in July 2005, a spokesperson before a blue backdrop with the bank emblem, journalists' recorders crowding the lectern; clean digital press photograph, cool white balance, sharp corporate lighting.

stations.label: MY OFFSHORE SELF IS BORN
stations.line: In Hong Kong I gained a second body — the CNH — and this is where the loneliness began, because now there were two of me, and one had to keep explaining the other to the world.
stations.era_photo: A Hong Kong bank branch in 2010 with a bright red 'RMB Deposits Available' banner in Chinese and English, Central district towers reflected in the glass; high-resolution digital photograph, glossy commercial colour, midday clarity.

stations.label: THE MORNING THE STORY CHANGED
stations.line: This is the wound, and I will not smooth it: the devaluation came without warning, my offshore price and my onshore price told different stories for three months, and I learned there are limits to how much I can manage the world's reading of me.
stations.era_photo: A trading desk at dawn in August 2015, multiple monitors flashing a sudden gap between CNY and CNH quotes, an analyst's hand frozen mid-gesture; digital DSLR photograph, screen-glow blue on a tired face, high ISO grain in the dark room.

stations.label: STILL STANDING ON THE BRIDGE
stations.line: Today I am the second-most-held reserve consideration few dared imagine in 1948, still translating between the house that made me and the world that watches — and I am, precisely, still here.
stations.era_photo: A modern Shanghai Bund view at dusk in 2024, Pudong skyline lit in gold and red, a woman in a tailored charcoal blazer looking out from a glass-walled office; ultra-sharp mirrorless digital photograph, cinematic HDR colour, reflections layered in the window.
